episode_title: Rune Ytreberg and Lars Adrian Giske: How iTromsø is Building an AI-Ready Foundation for News
transcript_file: 2025-03-03 - Rune Ytreberg and Lars Adrian Giske How iTromsø is Building an AI-Ready Foundation for News.txt
summary_claims: AI agents are not production-ready as of now; they take a lot of work and there are many steps where they can fail
Two and a half years of legal consultation were required before they could start building the centralized data platform due to GDPR sensitivity of joining personal-level datasets
OpenAI enterprise solution was too expensive for a small newspaper, creating a division where some journalists could make their own assistants but most could not
In eight months, referrals through ChatGPT went from zero to over a hundred thousand to news sites, citing Reuters data

guests.name: Rune Ytreberg
guests.role: Head of the Data Journalism Lab

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: You're a journalist. Nobody in the world knows better how to ask questions or structure questions or query data or query a source. And you have to think of it like that.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Reframes prompting as a natural extension of journalistic questioning skills. GIN adoption by journalists over fifty demonstrates AI augmenting rather than replacing human labor. The episode reinforces human-in-the-loop requirements, particularly regarding hallucination preventing public-facing AI deployment.
matches.matched_text: you're a journalist. Nobody in the world knows better how to ask questions or structure questions or query data or query a source. And you have to think of it like that.

matches.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: We barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kind of took a lot of our revenue... And with AI, and we have these new tech giants emerging, like you have OpenAI, you have Anthropic, you have Google coming in hard in the AI sphere, does increase competition. Because they are also informational ecosystems.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Hallucination is identified as the primary barrier preventing deployment of AI-interactive products to the public, directly linked to trust preservation. The episode also raises the risk of echo chambers from hyper-personalized news and the competitive threat from new informational ecosystems displacing traditional news platforms.
matches.matched_text: we barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kinda took a lot of our revenue that we were kind of relying on to do our jobs. And with AI, and we have these new tech giants emerging, like you have OpenAI, you have Anthropic, you have Google coming in hard. in the AI sphere, does increase competition. Because they are also informational ecosystems.

matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: They have to train while they are working. And I think that's the best way of understanding a new tool and understand how to use AI in your daily work is to actually try start using it in projects, in journalism which are important, because then it matters.
best_quote.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

matches.summary: Ethics is implicitly present through the editor-in-chief mandating AI projects and standardized system instruction hierarchies (16-20 steps covering accuracy, context, task, output format), but no formal ethics framework discussion. The approach is pragmatic and embedded in practice.
matches.matched_text: They have to train while they are working. And I think that's the best way of using a understand a new tool and understand how to use AI in your daily work is to actually try start using it in project um in journalism which are important because then then it matter.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: Our role will not as much be providing a platform like a website, but it will be providing data, like carefully curated and processed data... that helps people make sense of the world around them.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Rich technical depth: centralized data platform combining multiple public/private data sources with classical and generative AI; Kotemon as open-source RAG front-end solving citation problems other tools could not; graph RAG for entity relationships; open-source models via Ollama; multi-model approach; standardized prompt hierarchies. Also notes AI agents are not yet production-ready and newer models (O3) reduce prompting precision requirements.
matches.matched_text: Our role will not as much be providing a platform like a website, but it will be providing data, like carefully curated and processed data. That is kinda what's the word I'm looking for? Like when you net dough, you net data, right in such a way that it it helps people make sense of the world around them.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: They have to train while they are working. And I think that's the best way of understanding a new tool and understand how to use AI in your daily work is to actually try start using it in projects, in journalism which are important, because then it matters. Then you have to be sure that it doesn't hallucinate.
best_quote.speaker: Rune Ytreberg

matches.summary: A cornerstone literacy episode: training through real editorial projects rather than separate sessions; mini workshops pairing individual journalists with developers; editor-in-chief mandated participation; journalists over fifty successfully adopting GIN; prompting reframed as journalistic questioning; open-source tools (Kotemon) solving the cost barrier that split the newsroom when OpenAI enterprise was too expensive.
matches.matched_text: They have to train while they are working. And I think that's the best way of using a understand a new tool and understand how to use AI in your daily work is to actually try start using it in project um in journalism which are important because then then it matter. Then you have to be sure that it it doesn't hallucinate.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: If my end goal is having a system that is capable of saying that director A of company B had to sell their house because Company C went bankrupt. That kind of news notification is incredibly powerful for a journalist.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Demonstrates organizational transformation: developers embedded in newsroom attending morning meetings; editor-in-chief designating AI-supported project as top priority for six months; building tools with journalists not for them; aspiration to have 'most AI-competent newsroom in Norway before summer.' The organizational structure is purpose-built to integrate AI into editorial culture.
matches.matched_text: If my end goal is having a system that is capable of saying that director A Of company B had to sell their house because Company C went bankrupt. That kind of news notification is incredibly powerful for a journalist.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: We barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kind of took a lot of our revenue... And with AI, and we have these new tech giants emerging, like you have OpenAI, you have Anthropic, you have Google coming in hard in the AI sphere, does increase competition.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Explicit comparison between social media disruption and emerging AI disruption. ChatGPT referrals to news sites grew from zero to 100K in eight months. The episode frames AI tech companies as new informational ecosystems competing directly with news organizations, and advocates community engagement as a survival strategy.
matches.matched_text: we barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kinda took a lot of our revenue that we were kind of relying on to do our jobs. And with AI, and we have these new tech giants emerging, like you have OpenAI, you have Anthropic, you have Google coming in hard. in the AI sphere, does increase competition.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: Our role will not as much be providing a platform like a website, but it will be providing data, like carefully curated and processed data... that helps people make sense of the world around them.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Forward-looking vision of the newsroom shifting from website platform to curated data provider. Discusses the potential for hyper-personalized news delivery (with echo chamber risks) and the idea that the next reader may be an AI agent consuming structured data rather than a human reading articles.
matches.matched_text: Our role will not as much be providing a platform like a website, but it will be providing data, like carefully curated and processed data. That is kinda what's the word I'm looking for? Like when you net dough, you net data, right in such a way that it it helps people make sense of the world around them.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: We barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kind of took a lot of our revenue.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Cost constraints drive many decisions: OpenAI enterprise too expensive, requiring open-source alternatives; 25-person newsroom cannot afford dedicated training time. The episode positions curated local data as a competitive moat against tech giants and frames community engagement as an economic survival strategy.
matches.matched_text: we barely survived social media as an industry once Facebook and all these giants came and kinda took a lot of our revenue that we were kind of relying on to do our jobs.

matches.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: A good tip if you're going to build with infrastructure like this is call your lawyer first. That's like the golden rule of AI development and data journalism is like, can we actually use this data.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: GDPR compliance required two and a half years of legal consultation before building the centralized data platform. The legal/regulatory dimension is a practical constraint rather than a policy discussion.
matches.matched_text: a good tip if you're going to build with infrastructure like this is call your lawyer first. That's like the golden rule of AI. development and data journalism is like ca can we actually use this data.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: If my end goal is having a system that is capable of saying that director A of company B had to sell their house because Company C went bankrupt. That kind of news notification is incredibly powerful for a journalist. And it's very hard to do right now.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: GIN is now in production across the Laris media group; the centralized data platform is under active development. Mini workshops with individual journalists represent a workflow integration strategy. The episode shows both successful production deployments and ambitious systems still in development.
matches.matched_text: If my end goal is having a system that is capable of saying that director A Of company B had to sell their house because Company C went bankrupt. That kind of news notification is incredibly powerful for a journalist. And it's very hard to do right now.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: A good tip if you're going to build with infrastructure like this is call your lawyer first. That's like the golden rule of AI development and data journalism is like, can we actually use this data.
best_quote.speaker: Lars Adrian Giske

matches.summary: Central theme: joining personal-level datasets (property, tax, company, income) creates exponentially more sensitive data. Two and a half years of GDPR legal work preceded development. Granular access control and monitoring of journalist data access is required. The episode is the most detailed case study of data privacy challenges in AI-powered journalism across the corpus.
matches.matched_text: a good tip if you're going to build with infrastructure like this is call your lawyer first. That's like the golden rule of AI. development and data journalism is like ca can we actually use this data.
